approaches.name: Approach 1: Push Inefficient O(n)
approaches.summary: Make push operation costly by moving all elements from one queue to another to maintain LIFO order, then push the new element. Pop and top are O(1) as they access the front of the queue. Use this when push frequency is low.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Stack Using Two Queues with Push Inefficient Approach
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a stack using two queues where push operation is O(n) and pop/top are O(1).
ladders.explanation: This approach implements a stack (LIFO) using two queues (FIFO) by making the push operation costly. The key insight is that we can simulate LIFO behavior by rearranging elements between two queues during push operations. We maintain one primary queue (q1) that always represents the current stack order, with the top element at the front. When pushing a new element, we first move all existing elements from q1 to q2, then enqueue the new element to q1, and finally move all elements back from q2 to q1. This ensures the new element becomes the front of q1, making it accessible for pop/top operations in O(1) time. The intuition is that we're essentially rotating the queue to place new elements at the front, mimicking stack behavior. Time complexity: push O(n) due to element transfers, pop/top O(1). Space complexity: O(n) for storing elements in queues. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the push rearrangement subroutine from L1 as its core mechanism. Common mistakes include not properly handling empty stack cases, incorrect queue transfers, or forgetting to swap queue references. Real code pattern: Use two lists as queues with append for enqueue and pop(0) for dequeue.

Top: Peek front of q1: returns 2, q1=[2,1]
Pop: Dequeue from q1: returns 2, q1=[1]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Rearrange Queues During Push Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the core push logic that transfers elements between two queues to maintain LIFO order.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine handles the O(n) push operation by rearranging elements between two queues. When pushing a new element x, we need to make x the front of the primary queue (so it becomes the stack top). The algorithm: 1) Move all elements from q1 to q2 (emptying q1), 2) Enqueue x to q1 (now q1 contains only x at front), 3) Move all elements from q2 back to q1 (restoring order with x at front). The intuition is that we're creating space at the front of q1 for the new element by temporarily storing existing elements in q2. This ensures the most recently pushed element is always at the front of q1, enabling O(1) pop/top operations. Time complexity: O(n) where n is current stack size. Space complexity: O(n) for the two queues. Connection to L0: This is the heart of the push-inefficient approach used in the full stack implementation. Common mistakes include incorrect transfer orders, not handling empty queues properly, or inefficient double transfers. Real code pattern: Use while loops to transfer elements between queues.

examples.trace:
Start: q1=[2,1], q2=[]
Step 1: Move q1→q2: dequeue 2 from q1 to q2: q1=[1], q2=[2]; dequeue 1 from q1 to q2: q1=[], q2=[2,1]
Step 2: Enqueue 3 to q1: q1=[3], q2=[2,1]
Step 3: Move q2→q1: dequeue 2 from q2 to q1: q1=[3,2], q2=[1]; dequeue 1 from q2 to q1: q1=[3,2,1], q2=[]

Start: q1=[], q2=[]
Step 1: Move q1→q2: q1 empty, nothing to move: q1=[], q2=[]
Step 2: Enqueue 5 to q1: q1=[5], q2=[]
Step 3: Move q2→q1: q2 empty, nothing to move: q1=[5], q2=[]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining LIFO Order Using FIFO Queues
ladders.desc: Understand how to simulate stack behavior (LIFO) using queue operations (FIFO).
ladders.explanation: The central technique is using two FIFO queues to maintain LIFO order by strategically rearranging elements. The insight is that while a single queue naturally gives FIFO behavior, we can use a second queue as temporary storage to reverse the order during push operations. By always keeping the most recent element at the front of the primary queue, we can achieve stack-like behavior: pop/top simply access the front element. The rearrangement during push ensures new elements become the front, while existing elements maintain their relative order behind it. This works because queues only allow access at ends (enqueue at back, dequeue from front), so we need the two-queue dance to place new elements at the front. Time complexity analysis shows the tradeoff: O(n) push allows O(1) pop/top. Space complexity is O(n) across both queues. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented through the push rearrangement subroutine. Common mistakes include trying to use a single queue or misunderstanding the queue transfer pattern. Real code pattern: The key is maintaining invariant that primary queue front = stack top.

examples.input: Start with empty, push 1, push 2, push 3
examples.output: Queue state after pushes: front=3 (top), then 2, then 1
examples.trace:
After push 1: queue = [1] (front=1)
After push 2: Transfer: move 1 to temp, add 2, move 1 back → queue = [2,1] (front=2)
After push 3: Transfer: move 2,1 to temp, add 3, move 2,1 back → queue = [3,2,1] (front=3)
Result: Most recent (3) at front, maintaining LIFO order

examples.input: Queue [4,3,2,1] (front=4), push 5
examples.trace:
Current: queue front=4 (top), order: 4,3,2,1
Push 5: Move all elements to temp: temp gets 4,3,2,1
Add 5 to main queue: queue=[5]
Move elements back from temp: queue becomes [5,4,3,2,1]
Now front=5 (new top), preserving LIFO: 5 is most recent, then 4,3,2,1

testCases.input: Queue front=X, then Y, then Z, push W
testCases.expected: Queue front=W, then X, then Y, then Z

ladders.title: Building Block — Queue Transfer Between Two Queues
ladders.desc: Move all elements from one queue to another while preserving order.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves transferring all elements from a source queue to a destination queue. The algorithm is simple: while source queue is not empty, dequeue an element from source and enqueue it to destination. This preserves the FIFO order because we're moving elements in the same sequence they were in the source. The intuition is that queues maintain order, so transferring sequentially maintains that order. This operation is O(n) where n is number of elements transferred. Space complexity is O(1) extra space beyond the queues themselves. Connection to L2: This transfer operation is used twice in the push rearrangement to temporarily store elements. Common mistakes include not checking for empty queues, incorrect loop conditions, or modifying queues during transfer. Real code pattern: Use a while loop that continues until source is empty.

examples.input: Transfer from q1=[1,2,3] to q2=[]
examples.trace:
Start: q1=[1,2,3], q2=[]
Step 1: Dequeue 1 from q1, enqueue to q2: q1=[2,3], q2=[1]
Step 2: Dequeue 2 from q1, enqueue to q2: q1=[3], q2=[1,2]
Step 3: Dequeue 3 from q1, enqueue to q2: q1=[], q2=[1,2,3]

examples.input: Transfer from q1=[5] to q2=[10,20]
examples.trace:
Start: q1=[5], q2=[10,20]
Step 1: Dequeue 5 from q1, enqueue to q2: q1=[], q2=[10,20,5]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Enqueue and Dequeue
ladders.desc: Fundamental queue operations: adding to back (enqueue) and removing from front (dequeue).
ladders.explanation: This covers the basic FIFO queue operations. Enqueue adds an element to the back/rear of the queue. Dequeue removes and returns the element from the front of the queue. The intuition is that queues follow first-in-first-out order, like a real-world line. These operations are typically O(1) time for efficient implementations. Space complexity depends on the number of elements stored. Connection to L3: These operations are used in the queue transfer building block. Common mistakes include confusing front/back, trying to access middle elements, or not handling empty queues during dequeue. Real code pattern: Using list with append for enqueue and pop(0) for dequeue (though pop(0) is O(n); in practice use collections.deque for O(1) operations).

examples.input: Queue=[], enqueue 1, enqueue 2, dequeue, enqueue 3, dequeue
examples.output: First dequeue returns 1, second dequeue returns 2
examples.trace:
Start: queue=[]
Enqueue 1: queue=[1] (front=1, back=1)
Enqueue 2: queue=[1,2] (front=1, back=2)
Dequeue: Remove front (1), queue=[2], returns 1
Enqueue 3: queue=[2,3] (front=2, back=3)
Dequeue: Remove front (2), queue=[3], returns 2

Dequeue: Remove front (5), queue=[4], returns 5

Enqueue 7: queue=[6,7] (front=6, back=7)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding FIFO vs LIFO
ladders.desc: Basic difference between queue (FIFO) and stack (LIFO) data structures.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept explains the core difference between FIFO (First-In-First-Out) and LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) principles. A queue follows FIFO: the first element added is the first one removed, like people in a line. A stack follows LIFO: the last element added is the first one removed, like plates in a stack. The intuition is about order of access: queues process in arrival order, stacks process in reverse arrival order. Real-world analogies: queue = ticket line, stack = undo functionality. Time complexity for basic operations is typically O(1) for both. Space complexity is O(n) for storing elements. Connection to L4: Queue operations (enqueue/dequeue) implement FIFO behavior. Common mistakes include confusing which end is accessed or mixing up the principles. Real code pattern: Stack uses list with append/pop, queue uses collections.deque with append/popleft.

examples.input: Add 1, then 2, then 3 to both structures, then remove two elements
examples.output: Queue removes 1 then 2; Stack removes 3 then 2
examples.trace:
Queue (FIFO):
Add 1: queue=[1]
Add 2: queue=[1,2]
Add 3: queue=[1,2,3]
Remove: takes front (1), queue=[2,3]
Remove: takes front (2), queue=[3]

Stack (LIFO):
Push 1: stack=[1]
Push 2: stack=[1,2]
Push 3: stack=[1,2,3]
Pop: takes top (3), stack=[1,2]
Pop: takes top (2), stack=[1]

examples.input: Add A, B, C to queue and stack, remove all
examples.output: Queue removes A,B,C; Stack removes C,B,A
examples.trace:
Queue:
Enqueue A: [A]
Enqueue B: [A,B]
Enqueue C: [A,B,C]
Dequeue: removes A → [B,C]
Dequeue: removes B → [C]
Dequeue: removes C → []

Stack:
Push A: [A]
Push B: [A,B]
Push C: [A,B,C]
Pop: removes C → [A,B]
Pop: removes B → [A]
Pop: removes A → []

approaches.name: Approach 2: Pop Inefficient O(n)
approaches.summary: Make pop operation costly by moving all elements except the last one to another queue to simulate stack pop, then return the last element. Push and top are O(1) as they directly access the queue. Use this when pop frequency is low.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Stack Using Two Queues with Pop Inefficient Approach
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a stack using two queues where pop operation is O(n) by moving elements between queues.
ladders.explanation: This approach implements a stack using two queues by making the pop operation inefficient (O(n)) while keeping push and top operations efficient (O(1)). The key insight is that a queue follows FIFO (First-In-First-Out) order while a stack follows LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) order. To simulate stack behavior, we need to access the most recently added element first. We maintain two queues: q1 (main queue) and q2 (temporary queue). For push operations, we simply enqueue to q1. For top operations, we return the last element in q1. For pop operations, we need to access the last element, which requires moving all elements except the last one to q2, dequeuing the last element from q1, then swapping the queues so q2 becomes the new main queue. This approach is optimal when pop operations are infrequent compared to push/top operations. The intuition is that we're essentially 'rotating' the queue to bring the last element to the front for removal. Time complexity: push O(1), top O(1), pop O(n). Space complexity: O(n) for storing n elements across two queues. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the popHelper function from L1 as its core mechanism. Common mistakes include not properly swapping queues after pop, not handling empty stack cases, and incorrect queue operations order. Real code pattern: class StackUsingQueues: def __init__(self): self.q1 = Queue(), self.q2 = Queue(); def push(x): self.q1.enqueue(x); def pop(): if empty: return None; while len(self.q1) > 1: self.q2.enqueue(self.q1.dequeue()); popped = self.q1.dequeue(); self.q1, self.q2 = self.q2, self.q1; return popped; def top(): if empty: return None; return last element of q1; def empty(): return len(self.q1) == 0.

examples.input: push(1), push(2), push(3), pop(), push(4), pop(), pop(), pop()
examples.trace: Initialize: q1 = [], q2 = []. push(1): q1 = [1]. push(2): q1 = [1,2]. push(3): q1 = [1,2,3]. pop(): Move elements from q1 to q2 until last: q1 = [3], q2 = [1,2]. Pop 3 from q1: popped=3, q1=[], q2=[1,2]. Swap: q1=[1,2], q2=[]. Return 3. push(4): q1=[1,2,4]. pop(): Move: q1=[4], q2=[1,2]. Pop 4: popped=4, q1=[], q2=[1,2]. Swap: q1=[1,2], q2=[]. Return 4. pop(): Move: q1=[2], q2=[1]. Pop 2: popped=2, q1=[], q2=[1]. Swap: q1=[1], q2=[]. Return 2. pop(): Move: q1=[1], q2=[]. Pop 1: popped=1, q1=[], q2=[]. Swap: q1=[], q2=[]. Return 1.

examples.input: push(10), push(20), top(), pop(), push(30), top(), pop(), pop()
examples.trace: Initialize: q1=[], q2=[]. push(10): q1=[10]. push(20): q1=[10,20]. top(): Return last element 20. pop(): Move: q1=[20], q2=[10]. Pop 20: popped=20, q1=[], q2=[10]. Swap: q1=[10], q2=[]. Return 20. push(30): q1=[10,30]. top(): Return last element 30. pop(): Move: q1=[30], q2=[10]. Pop 30: popped=30, q1=[], q2=[10]. Swap: q1=[10], q2=[]. Return 30. pop(): Move: q1=[10], q2=[]. Pop 10: popped=10, q1=[], q2=[]. Swap: q1=[], q2=[]. Return 10.

push(1), push(2), push(3), push(4), pop(), pop(), push(5), pop(), pop(), pop()
push(0), push(1), push(2), pop(), push(3), pop(), pop(), pop()

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pop Helper Function with Queue Transfer
ladders.desc: Implement the core pop operation that moves elements between two queues to access the last element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the pop operation for our stack simulation. The function takes two queues as input: q1 (main queue containing elements) and q2 (temporary queue). The goal is to remove and return the last element from q1, which simulates stack's LIFO behavior. Since queues only allow access to the front element, we need to transfer all elements except the last one from q1 to q2. We do this by repeatedly dequeuing from q1 and enqueuing to q2 until only one element remains in q1. This last element is the one we want to pop (it was the most recently pushed). We dequeue it from q1, store it as the result, then swap q1 and q2 so that q2 (which now contains all remaining elements) becomes the new main queue, and q1 becomes empty for future operations. The intuition is that we're essentially 'rotating' the queue to bring the last element to the front. Time complexity: O(n) where n is number of elements in q1, as we process each element once. Space complexity: O(1) extra space beyond the queues. Connection to L0: This is the heart of the full solution's pop operation. Common mistakes include not handling empty queue case, incorrect loop condition (should be while len(q1) > 1, not while not empty), and forgetting to swap queues after operation. Real code pattern: def popHelper(q1, q2): if q1.empty(): return None; while q1.size() > 1: q2.enqueue(q1.dequeue()); popped = q1.dequeue(); q1, q2 = q2, q1; return popped.

examples.trace: Start: q1=[1,2,3], q2=[]. Check q1 not empty. While q1 size > 1 (3>1): Dequeue 1 from q1: q1=[2,3], enqueue to q2: q2=[1]. While q1 size > 1 (2>1): Dequeue 2 from q1: q1=[3], enqueue to q2: q2=[1,2]. Now q1 size = 1, exit loop. Dequeue last element from q1: popped=3, q1=[]. Swap q1 and q2: q1=[1,2], q2=[]. Return 3.
Start: q1=[10], q2=[]. Check q1 not empty. While q1 size > 1 (1>1 false): Skip loop. Dequeue last element from q1: popped=10, q1=[]. Swap q1 and q2: q1=[], q2=[]. Return 10.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Simulating LIFO with FIFO by Element Transfer
ladders.desc: Understand how moving elements between two queues allows accessing the last element first.
ladders.explanation: This core logic explains the fundamental technique of simulating Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) stack behavior using First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queues. The challenge is that queues only allow access to the front (oldest) element, but stacks need access to the back (newest) element. The solution is to use two queues and transfer elements between them. When we need to access the last element (for pop or top operations), we move all elements except the last one from the main queue to a temporary queue. This leaves the last element isolated in the main queue, where it can be accessed. After operation, we swap the queues so the temporary queue (containing all other elements) becomes the new main queue. The intuition is that we're essentially 'rotating' the queue: by repeatedly removing from front and adding to back of another queue, we're cycling through elements until we reach the desired one. This is similar to how you might search through a line of people by having them move to another line. Time complexity for this transfer: O(k) where k is number of elements transferred. Space complexity: O(1) extra space. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the popHelper function. Common mistakes include misunderstanding LIFO vs FIFO, incorrect transfer logic, and not realizing why two queues are needed (one queue alone cannot do this efficiently). Real code pattern: To get last element: while q1.size() > 1: q2.enqueue(q1.dequeue()); last = q1.dequeue(); then swap queues.

examples.input: Queue with elements [A,B,C] in FIFO order, need to access C (LIFO)
examples.output: Access C by transferring A and B
examples.trace: Start: q1=[A,B,C] (front=A, back=C). Goal: Access C (last element). Step 1: Transfer A from q1 to q2: dequeue A from q1 (now [B,C]), enqueue to q2 (now [A]). Step 2: Transfer B from q1 to q2: dequeue B from q1 (now [C]), enqueue to q2 (now [A,B]). Now q1 has only C. We can access C. After access, swap: q1 becomes [A,B], q2 becomes [].

examples.input: Queue [X,Y,Z,W], need to access W
examples.trace: Start: q1=[X,Y,Z,W]. Transfer X: q1=[Y,Z,W], q2=[X]. Transfer Y: q1=[Z,W], q2=[X,Y]. Transfer Z: q1=[W], q2=[X,Y,Z]. Now q1 has W. Access W. Swap: q1=[X,Y,Z], q2=[].

ladders.title: Building Block — Queue Operations and Two-Queue Management
ladders.desc: Master basic queue operations and how to maintain two queues for data transfer.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers the essential queue operations and the concept of using two queues together. A queue is a linear data structure that follows FIFO (First-In-First-Out) order. Basic operations include: enqueue(x) to add element x to the back, dequeue() to remove and return the front element, front() to view front element without removal, size() to get number of elements, and empty() to check if queue is empty. When using two queues, we typically designate one as the main queue (q1) that stores most elements, and another as temporary queue (q2) used during operations. The key management pattern is: during pop operation, we transfer elements from q1 to q2 until only one remains in q1, then we swap the queues so q2 becomes the new q1. This swapping is crucial because it maintains the invariant that q1 always contains all current stack elements after any operation. The intuition is that q2 acts as a 'holding area' during element transfer. Time complexity for individual queue operations: O(1) typically. Space complexity: O(n) across both queues. Connection to L2: This provides the operational foundation for the element transfer logic. Common mistakes include incorrect queue operation order, not handling empty queues, and misunderstanding which queue is active after operations. Real code pattern: class Queue: def enqueue(x): add to back; def dequeue(): remove from front; def size(): return length; def empty(): return size==0. For two queues: q1 = Queue(); q2 = Queue(); transfer: while q1.size() > 1: q2.enqueue(q1.dequeue()); then swap: q1, q2 = q2, q1.

examples.input: q1=[1,2,3], q2=[], transfer all but last from q1 to q2
examples.trace: Start: q1=[1,2,3], q2=[]. Check q1 size=3 > 1. Dequeue from q1: remove 1, q1=[2,3]. Enqueue to q2: add 1, q2=[1]. Check q1 size=2 > 1. Dequeue from q1: remove 2, q1=[3]. Enqueue to q2: add 2, q2=[1,2]. Check q1 size=1 > 1 false. Stop. Result: q1=[3], q2=[1,2].

examples.input: q1=[10,20], q2=[5], transfer all but last from q1 to q2
examples.trace: Start: q1=[10,20], q2=[5]. Check q1 size=2 > 1. Dequeue from q1: remove 10, q1=[20]. Enqueue to q2: add 10, q2=[5,10]. Check q1 size=1 > 1 false. Stop. Result: q1=[20], q2=[5,10].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Enqueue and Dequeue with FIFO Order
ladders.desc: Implement and understand fundamental queue operations: adding to back (enqueue) and removing from front (dequeue).
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the core queue operations that follow FIFO (First-In-First-Out) principle. Enqueue adds an element to the back (rear) of the queue. Dequeue removes and returns the element from the front (head) of the queue. These operations maintain the order: elements are processed in the same order they were added, like a real-world queue or line. For example, if we enqueue A, then B, then C, the queue order is [A,B,C] with A at front. Dequeue would remove A, leaving [B,C]. Another dequeue would remove B, leaving [C]. The intuition is that queues are fair - the first to arrive is first to be served. Time complexity: O(1) for both operations if implemented with efficient data structure like linked list or circular array. Space complexity: O(1) per element stored. Connection to L3: These are the building blocks for queue management. Common mistakes include confusing front/back, incorrect operation order, and not handling empty queue during dequeue. Real code pattern: class Queue: def __init__(self): self.items = []; def enqueue(x): self.items.append(x); def dequeue(): if self.empty(): return None; return self.items.pop(0); def empty(): return len(self.items)==0.

examples.input: Enqueue 5, Enqueue 10, Dequeue, Enqueue 15, Dequeue, Dequeue
examples.trace: Start: queue=[]. Enqueue 5: queue=[5]. Enqueue 10: queue=[5,10]. Dequeue: Remove front 5, queue=[10], return 5. Enqueue 15: queue=[10,15]. Dequeue: Remove front 10, queue=[15], return 10. Dequeue: Remove front 15, queue=[], return 15.

examples.input: Enqueue X, Enqueue Y, Dequeue, Enqueue Z, Dequeue, Dequeue
examples.trace: Start: queue=[]. Enqueue X: queue=[X]. Enqueue Y: queue=[X,Y]. Dequeue: Remove front X, queue=[Y], return X. Enqueue Z: queue=[Y,Z]. Dequeue: Remove front Y, queue=[Z], return Y. Dequeue: Remove front Z, queue=[], return Z.

testCases.input: Enqueue a, Enqueue b, Enqueue c, Dequeue, Dequeue
Enqueue 0, Enqueue 1, Enqueue 2, Dequeue, Enqueue 3, Dequeue
Enqueue x, Dequeue, Enqueue y, Dequeue
Enqueue 10, Enqueue 20, Enqueue 30, Dequeue, Dequeue, Enqueue 40, Dequeue
Enqueue p, Enqueue q, Dequeue, Enqueue r, Dequeue, Dequeue

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding FIFO vs LIFO Data Structures
ladders.desc: Learn the fundamental difference between queue (FIFO) and stack (LIFO) ordering principles.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept explains the core difference between two fundamental data structure ordering principles: FIFO (First-In-First-Out) and LIFO (Last-In-First-Out). FIFO, used by queues, means the first element added is the first one removed. Think of a real-world queue or line: people who arrive first get served first. LIFO, used by stacks, means the last element added is the first one removed. Think of a stack of plates: you add plates to the top and remove from the top. The key difference is the order of processing. For FIFO: if you add A, then B, then C, removal order is A, B, C. For LIFO: if you add A, then B, then C, removal order is C, B, A. This is crucial because many algorithms depend on which order elements are processed. The intuition is that FIFO is fair (first come first served) while LIFO is convenient for recent items (like undo operations). Time complexity for access: both can have O(1) operations. Space complexity: both O(n) for n elements. Connection to L4: This explains why queue operations work as they do. Common mistakes include confusing which end is accessed, mixing up terminology, and not understanding real-world analogies. Real code pattern: For FIFO queue: add to back, remove from front. For LIFO stack: add to top, remove from top.

examples.input: Add elements 1,2,3 in order, then remove all
examples.trace: FIFO (Queue): Start empty. Add 1: queue=[1]. Add 2: queue=[1,2]. Add 3: queue=[1,2,3]. Remove: takes front 1, queue=[2,3]. Remove: takes front 2, queue=[3]. Remove: takes front 3, queue=[]. Order: 1,2,3. LIFO (Stack): Start empty. Add 1: stack=[1]. Add 2: stack=[1,2]. Add 3: stack=[1,2,3]. Remove: takes top 3, stack=[1,2]. Remove: takes top 2, stack=[1]. Remove: takes top 1, stack=[]. Order: 3,2,1.

examples.input: Add A,B,C, remove one, add D, remove all
examples.trace: FIFO: Start empty. Add A: [A]. Add B: [A,B]. Add C: [A,B,C]. Remove: takes A, [B,C]. Add D: [B,C,D]. Remove all: takes B, then C, then D. Order: A,B,C,D but after first removal: A then remaining B,C,D gives A,B,C,D. Actually correct order: First removal gives A, then sequence gives B,C,D. So output A,B,C,D. Wait recalc: After add A,B,C: [A,B,C]. Remove one: takes A, left [B,C]. Add D: [B,C,D]. Remove all: takes B, then C, then D. So order: A,B,C,D. LIFO: Start empty. Add A: [A]. Add B: [A,B]. Add C: [A,B,C]. Remove: takes C, [A,B]. Add D: [A,B,D]. Remove all: takes D, then B, then A. Order: C,D,B,A.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Single Queue Rotation O(n)
approaches.summary: Use only one queue by rotating elements after each push to maintain LIFO order at the front. For push, add the element and then rotate the queue to move it to the front. Pop and top are O(1). Use this to simplify with one queue but still O(n) push.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Stack Using Single Queue with Rotation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a stack using only one queue where push rotates elements to maintain LIFO order.
ladders.explanation: This approach implements a stack using a single queue by rotating elements after each push operation to maintain Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) order. The key insight is that we can simulate stack behavior by ensuring the most recently pushed element is always at the front of the queue, ready for pop or top operations. For push(x): we enqueue x, then rotate the queue by dequeuing and re-enqueuing all previous elements (size-1 times). This moves x to the front. Pop simply dequeues the front element. Top returns the front element without removing it. Push becomes O(n) due to rotation, while pop and top remain O(1). Space is O(n) for storing elements. The intuition is that we're essentially reversing the queue order with each push to maintain stack semantics. This approach simplifies the two-queue method by using only one data structure while maintaining correct stack behavior. Common mistakes include forgetting to rotate after push (breaking LIFO), incorrect rotation count (should be size-1), or not handling empty stack cases. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the rotation subroutine with basic stack operations.

examples.input: Push 1, Push 2, Push 3, Pop, Top, Pop
examples.output: Pop returns 3, Top returns 2, Pop returns 2
examples.trace: Initial: queue = []. Push 1: queue = [1] (size=1, rotation 0 times). Push 2: queue = [1,2], rotate size-1=1 time: dequeue 1 → queue=[2], enqueue 1 → queue=[2,1]. Push 3: queue=[2,1,3], rotate size-1=2 times: dequeue 2 → queue=[1,3], enqueue 2 → queue=[1,3,2]; dequeue 1 → queue=[3,2], enqueue 1 → queue=[3,2,1]. Pop: dequeue front 3 → queue=[2,1], return 3. Top: return front 2, queue remains [2,1]. Pop: dequeue front 2 → queue=[1], return 2.

examples.input: Push 5, Push 6, Pop, Push 7, Pop, Pop
examples.output: First pop returns 6, second returns 7, third returns 5
examples.trace: Initial: queue = []. Push 5: queue = [5]. Push 6: queue = [5,6], rotate 1 time: dequeue 5 → queue=[6], enqueue 5 → queue=[6,5]. Pop: dequeue 6 → queue=[5], return 6. Push 7: queue = [5,7], rotate size-1=1 time: dequeue 5 → queue=[7], enqueue 5 → queue=[7,5]. Pop: dequeue 7 → queue=[5], return 7. Pop: dequeue 5 → queue=[], return 5.

testCases.input: Push 1, Push 2, Push 3, Pop, Pop, Pop
Push 5, Push 6, Push 7, Push 8, Pop, Pop, Push 9, Pop
Push 3, Push 4, Pop, Push 5, Pop, Pop
Push 0, Push 1, Push 2, Pop, Push 3, Pop, Pop, Pop
Push 9, Push 8, Push 7, Pop, Top, Push 6, Pop, Pop, Pop

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Queue Rotation After Push
ladders.desc: Implement the rotation operation that moves a newly pushed element to the front of the queue.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the single-queue stack approach. After pushing a new element to the queue, we need to rotate the queue so that this new element becomes the front element, ensuring LIFO order. The algorithm: 1. Get the current size of the queue (n). 2. Enqueue the new element x. 3. Perform rotation (n) times: dequeue an element from the front and immediately enqueue it to the back. However, note that after enqueuing x, the queue size becomes n+1. We need to rotate n times (not n+1) to move x to the front. Why n times? Initially, queue has n elements [a1,a2,...,an]. After enqueuing x: [a1,a2,...,an,x]. Rotating n times: first rotation moves a1 to back: [a2,...,an,x,a1]; second moves a2: [a3,...,an,x,a1,a2]; after n rotations, all original n elements are moved to back, leaving x at front: [x,a1,a2,...,an]. Time complexity is O(n) for the rotation. Space is O(1) extra. The intuition is circular shifting: we're essentially making the queue behave like a circular buffer where the new element becomes the new 'start'. Common mistakes: rotating n+1 times (which puts x at back again), or not counting size before enqueuing. This feeds into L0 as the critical step in the push operation.

examples.input: Queue = [1,2,3], push 4
examples.trace: Initial queue: [1,2,3], size n=3. Enqueue 4: [1,2,3,4]. Rotate n=3 times: 1) dequeue 1 → [2,3,4], enqueue 1 → [2,3,4,1]; 2) dequeue 2 → [3,4,1], enqueue 2 → [3,4,1,2]; 3) dequeue 3 → [4,1,2], enqueue 3 → [4,1,2,3]. Final: [4,1,2,3].

examples.input: Queue = [], push 5
examples.trace: Initial queue: [], size n=0. Enqueue 5: [5]. Rotate n=0 times (no rotation). Final: [5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining LIFO Order via Front Positioning
ladders.desc: The central technique of ensuring the most recent element is always at the queue front.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logic that enables a queue (FIFO) to behave like a stack (LIFO). The key insight is that if we can always keep the most recently added element at the front of the queue, then pop and top operations will correctly access the last-in element. In a normal queue, elements are removed from the front in the order they were added (FIFO). To achieve LIFO, we need to reverse this order dynamically. The technique: after each push, we manipulate the queue so that the new element becomes the front. This is done by rotating the queue—moving all existing elements to the back, bringing the new element to the front. Why does this work? Because a stack's essential property is that the last pushed item is the first popped. By ensuring the queue's front always holds the last pushed item, we simulate this property. The algorithm maintains this invariant: after any sequence of operations, the queue front is the top of the stack. Time analysis: maintaining this invariant requires O(n) work per push. Space is O(n). The intuition is like constantly reordering a line so the newest person goes to the front. Common mistakes include not maintaining the invariant after every push, or misunderstanding that pop/top don't need rotation. This feeds into L1 as the rationale for the rotation subroutine.

examples.input: Start empty, push A, push B
examples.trace: After push A: queue = [A] (front=A). Push B: enqueue B → [A,B], rotate to move B to front: dequeue A → [B], enqueue A → [B,A]. Now front=B (last pushed).

examples.input: Queue [X,Y,Z], push W
examples.trace: Initial: front=X. Push W: enqueue W → [X,Y,Z,W], rotate size-1=3 times: 1) dequeue X → [Y,Z,W], enqueue X → [Y,Z,W,X]; 2) dequeue Y → [Z,W,X], enqueue Y → [Z,W,X,Y]; 3) dequeue Z → [W,X,Y], enqueue Z → [W,X,Y,Z]. Now front=W.

testCases.input: Queue [a], push b, what is front?
After pushes x,y,z, pop, then push w, what is front?
Queue [p], push q, pop, push r, what is front?
Queue [1,2], push 3, pop, what was front before pop?
Queue [100], push 200, push 300, what is front?
Empty, push 0, push 1, pop, push 2, what is front?

ladders.title: Building Block — Queue Operations: Enqueue and Dequeue
ladders.desc: Implement and understand basic queue operations used in the stack simulation.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental queue operations required to build the stack. A queue is a linear data structure that follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order. The two core operations are: enqueue(x) adds element x to the back (rear) of the queue, and dequeue() removes and returns the element from the front of the queue. Additional operations like peek() (return front without removal) and size() (return number of elements) are also useful. In the context of implementing a stack, we use these operations to manipulate the queue: enqueue to add new elements, and dequeue during rotation to move elements from front to back. The algorithm assumes a standard queue interface. Time complexity: enqueue and dequeue are typically O(1) for linked-list or circular array implementations. Space is O(n) for storing elements. The intuition is that queues naturally handle elements in arrival order, but we'll use them in a non-standard way to achieve LIFO. Common mistakes include using stack operations directly (not allowed), confusing front/back, or not handling empty queue cases in dequeue. This feeds into L2 as the tools needed to implement the front-positioning logic.

examples.input: Enqueue 5, Enqueue 10, Dequeue, Enqueue 15, Dequeue
examples.trace: Start: queue=[]. Enqueue 5: queue=[5]. Enqueue 10: queue=[5,10]. Dequeue: remove front 5 → queue=[10], return 5. Enqueue 15: queue=[10,15]. Dequeue: remove front 10 → queue=[15], return 10.

examples.input: Enqueue A, Enqueue B, Peek, Dequeue, Peek
examples.output: First peek returns A, second peek returns B
examples.trace: Start: queue=[]. Enqueue A: queue=[A]. Enqueue B: queue=[A,B]. Peek: return front A, queue remains [A,B]. Dequeue: remove A → queue=[B], return A. Peek: return front B, queue remains [B].

testCases.input: Enqueue a, Enqueue b, Enqueue c, Dequeue, Dequeue
Enqueue p, Enqueue q, Dequeue, Enqueue r, Dequeue
Enqueue z, Peek, Dequeue, Peek (on empty)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Adding and Removing Elements from a List
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental list operations that underlie queue behavior.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the basic list operations that implement queue functionality. In many programming languages, a queue can be simulated using a list with specific access patterns. The key operations are: append(x) adds x to the end of the list (simulating enqueue), and pop(0) removes and returns the first element of the list (simulating dequeue). Also, list[0] accesses the first element without removal (peek), and len(list) gives the size. The algorithm uses these to manage elements in FIFO order. For example, to enqueue, we append to the list; to dequeue, we pop from index 0. Time complexity: append is O(1) amortized, but pop(0) is O(n) in many implementations because it requires shifting elements. In practice, we might use collections.deque in Python for O(1) operations. Space is O(n). The intuition is that a list is a sequential container, and by restricting ourselves to adding at one end and removing from the other, we get queue behavior. Common mistakes include using pop() without index (removes from end, not front), or inserting at front (breaks FIFO). This feeds into L3 as the concrete implementation of queue operations.

examples.input: List = [], append 3, append 5, pop(0), append 7, pop(0)
examples.trace: Start: list=[]. Append 3: list=[3]. Append 5: list=[3,5]. Pop(0): remove first element 3 → list=[5], return 3. Append 7: list=[5,7]. Pop(0): remove first element 5 → list=[7], return 5.

examples.input: List = [10], append 20, list[0], pop(0), list[0]
examples.trace: Start: list=[10]. Append 20: list=[10,20]. List[0]: access first element 10, list unchanged. Pop(0): remove 10 → list=[20], return 10. List[0]: access first element 20, list unchanged.

testCases.input: Append a, Pop(0), Append b, Pop(0)
Append x, Append y, Append z, Pop(0), Pop(0)
Append p, Append q, Pop(0), Append r, Pop(0)
Append z, List[0], Pop(0), List[0] (on empty)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding FIFO vs LIFO Order
ladders.desc: Learn the basic difference between queue (FIFO) and stack (LIFO) data structures.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level explains the core concepts of FIFO (First-In-First-Out) and LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) ordering. A queue is a FIFO data structure: the first element added is the first one removed, like a line of people waiting. A stack is a LIFO data structure: the last element added is the first one removed, like a stack of plates. Key analogy: FIFO is a 'queue' (British line), LIFO is a 'stack' (pile). Operations: for queue, we add (enqueue) to the back and remove (dequeue) from the front; for stack, we add (push) to the top and remove (pop) from the top. The problem challenges us to implement a stack (LIFO) using only queue operations (FIFO), which seems contradictory because their orders are opposite. The intuition for the solution is that we can manipulate the queue to reverse the order dynamically. Time and space are not primary here; focus is on conceptual understanding. Common mistakes include confusing which end is which, or thinking stack and queue are the same. This feeds into L4 as the motivation for using list operations in a specific way to achieve FIFO behavior.

examples.input: FIFO: add A, add B, remove. LIFO: add A, add B, remove.
examples.output: FIFO removes A, LIFO removes B
examples.trace: FIFO (queue): Start empty. Add A: queue=[A]. Add B: queue=[A,B]. Remove: takes from front, so remove A → queue=[B]. LIFO (stack): Start empty. Add A: stack=[A]. Add B: stack=[A,B] (B on top). Remove: takes from top, so remove B → stack=[A].

examples.input: Add 1, add 2, add 3, remove, remove for both FIFO and LIFO
examples.output: FIFO removes 1 then 2, LIFO removes 3 then 2
examples.trace: FIFO: Start empty. Add 1: [1]. Add 2: [1,2]. Add 3: [1,2,3]. Remove: front 1 → [2,3]. Remove: front 2 → [3]. LIFO: Start empty. Add 1: [1]. Add 2: [1,2]. Add 3: [1,2,3]. Remove: top 3 → [1,2]. Remove: top 2 → [1].

testCases.input: FIFO: add X, add Y, remove
LIFO: add X, add Y, remove
FIFO: add a, add b, add c, remove, remove
LIFO: add a, add b, add c, remove, remove
FIFO: add 1, remove, add 2, add 3, remove
LIFO: add 1, remove, add 2, add 3, remove
FIFO: add p, add q, remove, add r, remove
LIFO: add p, add q, remove, add r, remove
